If this rain doesn't let up soon, the bottom acreage is going to be completely underwater.
I've got to get the livestock moved to the high ridge before the creek overflows its banks.
All that hard work tilling the soil is just gone now that the topsoil has washed away.
The wind took out the north fence line and half the corn is flattened in the field.
I don't know how I'm going to pay back the seed loan with a crop like this.
I don't care what those lights are, as long as they don't scorch my winter wheat.
The cows have gone completely silent and won't budge an inch toward the milking parlor.
If that strange glow keeps the frost away, it might just save my late harvest.
I've spent forty years watching these skies, but I've never seen the clouds turn that color.
My father always told me to trust the land, but the land is shaking beneath my boots.
That fence line has been sitting right there since my father first cleared this acreage.
You can't just push your grazing land three feet into my prime corn soil.
My livestock are getting into your pasture because that old cedar post finally rotted through.
I don't care what the new survey says when the creek has shifted the bank.
We need to settle this now before the planting season starts and the tractors move in.
I don't care where that swirling light comes from, it's trampling my best crop of winter wheat.
If this hole in the air keeps sucking up the topsoil, I'll never get the north field planted.
I've lived on this land forty years and I ain't never seen a fence line warp like that.
I hope whatever is on the other side of that glow doesn't have a taste for livestock.
This strange shimmer is right in the middle of the pasture where the cattle need to graze.
This soil is far too acidic and choked with rot to ever grow a decent crop of corn.
I need to find a clearing with enough sunlight to keep my seedlings from stretching and dying.
These thick vines are strangling the native brush just like the bindweed does to my south pasture.
My boots are sinking into this muck, and I can tell the drainage here is absolutely nonexistent.
If I can just find a steady stream, I might be able to irrigate a small plot of land.
The wind has stopped blowing and the birds have gone quiet across the north pasture.
I can hear the soil shifting beneath my boots while the world holds its breath.
This heavy stillness makes me wonder if the rain will ever break this dry spell.
There is nothing but the sound of my own breathing while I watch the corn.
I have spent many hours in the fields, but this kind of silence feels unnatural.
You get off my acreage right now before I call the sheriff to haul you away.
I have worked this soil for forty years and I won't let you burn my barns.
Put that weapon down or you'll find out how I handle pests in my cornfields.
My livestock is terrified and I will do whatever it takes to protect my herd.
You think you can bully me, but you're standing on the land that feeds this town.
This old John Deere tractor has pulled my plows through the toughest clay for twenty years.
My grandfather handed me these heirloom seeds, and they produce the sweetest corn in the county.
This weathered leather saddle fits my best horse perfectly and has seen a thousand morning chores.
The irrigation system I built by hand ensures my crops survive even the hottest August droughts.
I keep my grandfather's heavy iron skillet because it sears the meat just right after a harvest.
The creek is running low this July, and I have to decide which fields get watered first.
I only have enough seed left in the bin to plant half of the north acreage.
These bags of fertilizer are costing me a fortune, but the soil is completely depleted.
I need to rotate the cattle to the lower pasture before they overgraze the remaining grass.
With the rain delaying the harvest, I am worried the grain will rot before I get it in.
